key_insight: MinSurf systematically resolves mineral surface stability at the level of atomic terminations, linking surface energies to equilibrium morphologies rather than relying on facet-level assumptions.
engineering_relevance: The framework helps engineers choose physically representative mineral surface models for faster, more reliable simulations in carbon mineralization, geo-energy storage, catalysis, and electrochemical interfaces.
method: Enumerate candidate surface slabs and oriented unit cells across mineral crystal structures.
Label a subset with DFT, then train a machine-learning interatomic potential (MinNEP) to predict surface energies.
Use Wulff construction to translate surface-energy rankings into equilibrium crystal morphologies.
evidence_signal: Across ten representative minerals, MinSurfSet includes 764 surface slabs and MinNEP achieves a mean absolute error of 0.0119 eV/Å² with an overall speedup of 1.14×10^4 over DFT, while preserving the DFT-derived facet hierarchy.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and coverage of the enumerated training surfaces, so rare terminations or minerals outside the benchmark set may require additional DFT labeling.
paper_title: MinSurf: resolving the atomic-scale stability landscape of mineral surfaces
